commands.name: stats
commands.description: Calculates aggregate statistics. The most performance-efficient aggregation command — Splunk can push computation to indexers.
examples: | stats count by src_ip, dest_ip, protocol
| stats sum(bytes) as total_bytes, count by src_ip
| stats avg(cpu_load) as avg_cpu, max(cpu_load) as peak_cpu by host
| stats dc(user) as unique_users by host
| stats latest(Status) as Status, latest(NodeName) as Name by NodeDNS
tips: Place stats as early as possible to reduce event volume sent to search head
Prefer stats over eventstats when you only need the aggregated result
Use dc() instead of values() when you only need a count of unique values

commands.name: search
commands.description: Filters events matching an expression. When used as the first command (no pipe), it is the most efficient filter — evaluated at the indexer level.
tips: Inline filters on the base search line are faster than post-pipe search
Use field=value format in the base search to leverage index-time fields
Wildcards at the start of a value (e.g., *admin) defeat term indexing

commands.name: where
commands.description: Filters events using an eval expression. Evaluated after extraction, so less efficient than inline search. Use for numeric comparisons and complex logic.
tips: Prefer inline field=value filters over | where when possible
Use | where for numeric range comparisons (count > 100)
| where action=failure (no quotes) uses search-mode matching, which is case-insensitive

commands.name: eval
commands.description: Creates or modifies fields using expressions. Supports arithmetic, string functions, conditionals, and conversion functions.

functions.desc: True if IP is in CIDR range

tips: Chain multiple evals in one command: | eval a=x, b=y
Use coalesce() to handle multiple possible field names (e.g., user vs username)
eval runs after extraction — consider rex for pattern-based extraction instead

commands.name: sort
commands.description: Sorts results. Prefix with - for descending (default), + for ascending. Limit reduces I/O.
tips: Always include a limit with sort (e.g., sort 1000 -count) to avoid sorting billions of events
sort -count is most common — highest-count items first

commands.name: table
commands.description: Retains only specified fields and displays them as a table. Use at the end of a search to clean up output.
examples: | table src_ip, dest_ip, bytes, protocol, action
tips: Use table at the very end to select which fields to display
table removes all other fields — use fields instead if you need to keep others

commands.name: fields
commands.description: Keeps (+) or removes (-) specified fields. Removing unneeded fields early improves performance by reducing data passed to subsequent commands.
tips: Use '| fields - _raw' to strip raw event data and speed up downstream commands
Drop fields early in the pipeline to reduce memory usage

commands.name: rename
commands.description: Renames fields for cleaner output or to resolve naming conflicts.
examples: | rename src_ip AS "Source IP", dest_ip AS "Destination IP"
tips: Use rename before table to display human-friendly column headers

commands.name: rex
commands.description: Extracts fields using named capture groups in a regular expression. More flexible than transforms.conf for ad-hoc extraction.
tips: Named groups (?<fieldname>...) create new fields
Use field= to target a specific field rather than _raw
For repeated patterns use mode=sed for substitution

commands.name: timechart
commands.description: Creates a time-series chart. Ideal for trending data over time. Each unique value in the 'by' field becomes a separate series.
tips: Adjust span to match your time range (e.g., span=5m for last hour, span=1h for last day)
Limit 'by' field cardinality — too many series makes charts unreadable
Use | timechart limit=10 to cap the number of series displayed

commands.name: dedup
commands.description: Removes duplicate events based on specified field values. Optionally keeps N copies per unique combination.
tips: dedup is expensive on large datasets — consider stats dc() instead
Use sortby to control which duplicate is kept (e.g., most recent)

commands.name: top
commands.description: Returns the top N most common values of a field, with count and percentage. Shortcut for a common stats + sort pattern.
tips: top is a shortcut for | stats count by field | sort -count | head N
Default limit is 10 — always specify limit for clarity

commands.name: rare
commands.description: Returns the least common values. Useful for detecting anomalous or infrequent events.
tips: Useful for security investigations — rare processes, users, or IPs stand out as anomalies

commands.name: lookup
commands.description: Enriches events by joining with a CSV lookup file or a Splunk lookup definition. Adds context from external data sources.
examples: | lookup geoip src_ip OUTPUT country, city, lat, lon
| lookup asset_inventory host OUTPUT owner, department, criticality
| lookup user_directory username OUTPUT full_name, email, manager
tips: Lookups are evaluated at the search head — place them after stats to reduce event count
Use OUTPUT (not OUTPUTNEW) to overwrite existing field values
Use OUTPUTNEW to only populate fields when they don't already exist

commands.name: transaction
commands.description: Groups events into transactions based on shared field values and time proximity. Use sparingly — extremely resource intensive.
tips: transaction is very expensive — prefer stats or streamstats when possible
Always set maxspan to prevent runaway transactions
Consider using 'stats list' as a lighter alternative

commands.name: eventstats
commands.description: Like stats, but retains all original events and adds aggregate fields to each event. Useful for computing per-group statistics alongside raw events.
examples: | eventstats avg(response_time) as avg_rt by host
| eventstats count as group_count by src_ip
tips: eventstats is more memory-intensive than stats — use stats when you don't need raw events
Common pattern: | eventstats avg(field) as avg | where field > avg * 2

commands.name: streamstats
commands.description: Running/rolling statistics as events are processed in time order. Useful for cumulative totals and moving averages.
examples: | streamstats window=10 avg(bytes) as moving_avg_bytes by src_ip
| streamstats sum(count) as cumulative_events by action
tips: Use window= for moving/rolling calculations (e.g., 10-event moving average)
Without window=, streamstats computes cumulative running totals
Requires events to be sorted by _time first

commands.name: appendpipe
commands.description: Appends results of a sub-search to current results. Useful for adding totals rows or combining multiple query perspectives.
examples: | appendpipe [ stats count as count, "TOTAL" as src_ip ]
| appendpipe [ where action="denied" | stats count as blocked ]
tips: Use for adding summary/total rows to a table
Avoid in performance-critical searches — it runs a second search

commands.name: head / tail
commands.description: head returns the first N events; tail returns the last N events (by current sort order). Default N is 10.
tips: Always use head after sort to limit output efficiently
head is evaluated at the search head — use limit=N in the base search for indexer-level limiting

commands.name: bucket
commands.description: Groups continuous values (especially _time) into discrete buckets. Alias: bin.
examples: | bucket span=5m _time | stats count by _time, action
tips: Use before stats to enable time-series grouping
timechart is a simpler shortcut when working with _time buckets

commands.name: iplocation
commands.description: Adds geolocation fields (Country, City, Region, lat, lon) for an IP address field using the built-in Splunk MaxMind database.
examples: | iplocation src_ip | stats count by Country
tips: iplocation only works for public IPs — private IPs return empty values
Splunk's built-in database may be outdated — consider a custom lookup for precision

commands.name: inputlookup
commands.description: Returns all rows from a lookup file as search results, without requiring a base search.
examples: | inputlookup blocked_ips | fields ip, reason, added_date
tips: Use as a starting point when you want to query a lookup table directly
Combine with | join or | appendcols to merge with search results

commands.name: join
commands.description: Joins results with a sub-search on matching field values. Functionally similar to SQL JOIN. Expensive — prefer lookup for static enrichment.
examples: | join src_ip [ search index=threat_intel | fields ip, threat_score ]
| join type=left user [ inputlookup user_directory | fields username, dept ]
tips: join is expensive — prefer | lookup for static data enrichment
Default type is inner — use left to keep events with no match
Sub-search is limited to 50,000 results by default

commands.name: makeresults
commands.description: Generates N empty result rows. Used for testing pipelines, building dashboards, or creating synthetic data.
tips: Useful for debugging eval expressions without running a real search

commands.name: map
commands.description: Runs a sub-search for each result row, substituting field values. Similar to a loop. Very expensive — use sparingly.
examples: | map maxsearches=5 search="search index=os host=$host$ | stats count"
tips: map is extremely resource intensive — limit with maxsearches
Prefer | join or | lookup when possible

commands.name: predict
commands.description: Uses machine learning to predict future values of a time-series field. Requires the Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK) or built-in algorithms.
examples: | timechart span=1h count | predict count future_timespan=24h
| timechart span=1d sum(bytes) as daily_bytes | predict daily_bytes
tips: Requires a timechart or bucket + stats pipeline as input
Use for capacity planning or anomaly baselining

commands.name: anomalydetection
commands.description: Identifies anomalous events using statistical thresholds. Marks events as anomalous based on deviation from expected patterns.
examples: | stats count by src_ip | anomalydetection count
tips: Requires numeric input — compute a stat first (count, avg, etc.)
Higher threshold = fewer anomalies flagged

optimization_rules.title: Wildcard Index
optimization_rules.description: Wildcard indexes search across multiple indexes, increasing I/O significantly.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Specify an exact index (e.g., index=netops) or list specific indexes (index=netops OR index=netfw).

optimization_rules.title: No Time Constraint
optimization_rules.description: Searches without an earliest= constraint scan all data — avoid in production.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Always add earliest=-1h (or appropriate range) to the base search.

optimization_rules.title: Wide Time Range (7d+)
optimization_rules.description: Searching 7+ days dramatically increases data scanned and search duration.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Narrow to the minimum needed time window for the investigation.

optimization_rules.title: No Field Filters
optimization_rules.description: Without field filters (user=, host=, src_ip=, etc.), every event in the index is evaluated.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Add at least one indexed field filter in the base search line before the first pipe.

optimization_rules.title: Leading Wildcard
optimization_rules.description: Values like *admin or *error cannot use term indexes — they force a full scan of every event.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Use trailing wildcards only (admin* or error*), or use | rex for regex extraction.

optimization_rules.title: Transaction Command
optimization_rules.description: | transaction is one of the most resource-intensive commands. It buffers all events in memory.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Replace with | stats list() for grouping, or | streamstats for running computations.
optimization_rules.example_fix: | stats list(action) as actions, count by session_id

optimization_rules.title: Searching _raw
optimization_rules.description: Using | search or | where on _raw scans full raw event text instead of extracted fields.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Extract fields with | rex first, then filter on the extracted field.

optimization_rules.title: Filter After Heavy Processing
optimization_rules.description: Placing | where or | search after expensive commands (transaction, stats) means processing more data than necessary.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Move field filters to the base search line before any pipes.
optimization_rules.example_fix: Add filter conditions directly to the index=... line

optimization_rules.title: Large Sub-search
optimization_rules.description: Sub-searches (brackets) are limited to 50,000 results and run in memory on the search head.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Use | join or | lookup for large datasets instead of sub-searches.

optimization_rules.title: No Sourcetype Filter
optimization_rules.description: Without sourcetype=, all data in the index is evaluated regardless of format.
optimization_rules.recommendation: Always specify sourcetype= to enable sourcetype-level indexing acceleration.

query_templates.name: Top Network Talkers
query_templates.description: Find the top source/destination IP pairs by traffic volume
query_templates.spl:
index=netops sourcetype=cisco:ios earliest=-1h
| stats count, sum(bytes) as total_bytes by src_ip, dest_ip, protocol
| sort -total_bytes
| head 20
| eval mb = round(total_bytes/1048576, 2)
| table src_ip, dest_ip, protocol, count, mb

query_templates.name: Firewall Denied Traffic
query_templates.description: Show all denied/blocked connections sorted by frequency
query_templates.spl:
index=netfw sourcetype=cisco:asa earliest=-4h
| stats count by src_ip, dest_ip, dest_port, action
| where action="denied" OR action="blocked"
| sort -count
| head 50

query_templates.name: Failed Login Attempts
query_templates.description: Detect brute-force attempts by counting auth failures per user and IP
query_templates.spl:
index=os sourcetype=linux_syslog earliest=-1h
| stats count by user, src_ip, action
| where action="failure" OR action="failed"
| sort -count
| where count > 5

query_templates.name: Windows Failed Authentication (Event 4625)
query_templates.description: Windows login failures grouped by account and source
query_templates.spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" earliest=-24h
EventCode=4625
| stats count by user, src_ip, Logon_Type, host
| sort -count

query_templates.name: SolarWinds Node Status Report
query_templates.description: Current status of all monitored nodes from Orion
query_templates.spl:
index=solarwinds* sourcetype=solarwinds:orion earliest=-15m
| stats latest(Status) as Status, latest(NodeName) as NodeName by host, NodeDNS, SiteID
| eval status_label = case(Status=="Up","🟢 Up", Status=="Down","🔴 Down", 1==1,"🟡 Unknown")
| sort NodeName
| table NodeName, NodeDNS, SiteID, status_label

query_templates.name: SolarWinds Active Alerts
query_templates.description: Recent Orion alert events with node context
query_templates.spl:
index=solarwinds* sourcetype=solarwinds:orion earliest=-1h
| stats count by NodeName, NodeDNS, SiteID, AlertMessage
| sort -count
| table NodeName, NodeDNS, SiteID, AlertMessage, count

query_templates.name: SolarWinds Interface Traffic
query_templates.description: Network interface utilization from Orion pollers
query_templates.spl:
index=solarwinds* sourcetype=solarwinds:orion earliest=-1h
| stats count, sum(ifInOctets) as in_bytes, sum(ifOutOctets) as out_bytes by host, InterfaceAlias
| eval in_mb = round(in_bytes/1048576, 2)
| eval out_mb = round(out_bytes/1048576, 2)
| sort -in_bytes
| table host, InterfaceAlias, in_mb, out_mb, count

query_templates.name: Web Application Error Rates
query_templates.description: HTTP error rate by URI path over time
query_templates.spl:
index=web sourcetype=access_combined earliest=-4h
| eval is_error = if(status >= 400, 1, 0)
| stats count as total, sum(is_error) as errors by uri_path
| eval error_pct = round((errors/total)*100, 1)
| where total > 10
| sort -error_pct
| head 25

query_templates.name: Okta MFA Events
query_templates.description: MFA success/failure events grouped by user
query_templates.spl:
index=okta sourcetype=OktaIM2:log earliest=-24h
| where eventType LIKE "user.mfa%"
| stats count by user, outcome, eventType
| sort -count

query_templates.name: CrowdStrike Detections
query_templates.description: Recent detection events with severity and host context
query_templates.spl:
index=crowdstrike sourcetype=crowdstrike:fdr earliest=-24h
ComputerName=*
| stats count by hostname, UserName, FileName, Severity, PatternDispositionDescription
| sort -count
| head 50

query_templates.name: VMware VM Events
query_templates.description: vSphere VM lifecycle events (power on/off, vMotion, snapshot)
query_templates.spl:
index=vmware sourcetype=vmware:esxi:hostd earliest=-24h
| stats count by vm_name, event_type, host
| sort -count

query_templates.name: Top Bandwidth Consumers
query_templates.description: Top IP addresses by total bandwidth consumed
query_templates.spl:
index=netops earliest=-1h
| eval mb = round(bytes/1048576, 2)
| stats sum(mb) as total_mb by src_ip, dest_ip
| sort -total_mb
| head 20
| rename total_mb AS "Total MB"

query_templates.name: Lateral Movement Detection
query_templates.description: Detect accounts connecting to many distinct hosts (potential lateral movement)
query_templates.spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" earliest=-4h
EventCode=4624
| stats dc(host) as unique_hosts, values(host) as hosts by user
| where unique_hosts > 5
| sort -unique_hosts

query_templates.name: Unusual DNS Activity
query_templates.description: Hosts with abnormally high DNS query volume (C2 beaconing indicator)
query_templates.spl:
index=netops sourcetype=stream:dns earliest=-1h
| stats count by src_ip, query
| where count > 100
| sort -count
| head 50

common_errors.cause: The specified index has not been created in Splunk or you lack access.
common_errors.fix: Verify the index name with your Splunk admin. Common indexes: main, netops, os, wineventlog, web.

common_errors.cause: Typo in command name or app not installed.
common_errors.fix: Check spelling: stats (not stat), where (not filter), timechart (not timegraph).

common_errors.cause: Field name in 'by' clause doesn't exist in the results.
common_errors.fix: Check that the field is being extracted. Use | fields to see available fields.

common_errors.cause: Search returns millions of events before the pipe.
common_errors.fix: Add more filters to the base search: specific host=, user=, or narrow earliest= range.

common_errors.cause: Missing time bound, wildcard index, or expensive command (transaction, join).
common_errors.fix: Add earliest=, narrow index/sourcetype, and replace transaction with stats.
